label: ai
topic: Identity shaped by relationships with women — sister, exchange students, a refugee friend
text:
My sister taught me bravery before I had a word for it. She was four years older, which at the ages we were — she twelve, I eight — constituted an unbridgeable gulf of sophistication. She wore black nail polish and once told our uncle, at Thanksgiving dinner, that his opinion about women's soccer was statistically incorrect. The table went silent. She kept eating her green beans.

I studied her the way you study anyone who seems to have solved a problem you haven't yet identified. The problem, I would later understand, was how to exist in a room without apologising for it. Emma did not apologise. She entered rooms like she had been invited, even when she hadn't. I wanted to be her. Instead, I was myself — tentative, watchful, a girl who rehearsed sentences in her head and then decided not to say them.

The first crack in my silence came from an unexpected direction. The year I turned fourteen, our family hosted an exchange student from São Paulo named Lucia. She was seventeen, tall, with curly hair she was constantly pushing out of her face, and she cried openly and without embarrassment, which was the most foreign thing about her. She cried at movies, at songs, at a story our neighbour told about losing her cat. In my family, crying was something you did alone, in the shower, with the water running.

"Why do you do that?" I asked her once. We were watching a nature documentary. A baby elephant had been separated from its mother.

"Because it is sad," she said, as if this were a complete explanation. And maybe it was.

Lucia taught me vulnerability. Not as weakness — she was one of the strongest people I had ever met, navigating a foreign country and a host family's bewildering customs with grace. But she refused to pretend she was not affected by things. She wore her emotional life on the outside, and the world did not end. If anything, people moved closer.

I started letting myself feel things in front of other people. Small concessions at first: admitting I was sad about a grade, letting my eyes water at a funeral instead of staring at a fixed point on the wall.

Then there was Amira. She arrived in our school district the autumn of my junior year, a refugee from Syria who had spent two years in a camp in Jordan. She spoke careful, precise English learned from American television, and she had a quietness I recognised — not the quietness of having nothing to say but of someone who has learned that speaking can be dangerous.

We became friends over a shared lunch table. Amira did not talk about what she had been through unless she chose to. Instead, she told me about her grandmother's garden in Aleppo, the jasmine along the wall, the way light fell through the kitchen window. She talked about small, beautiful things with a tenderness that felt like resistance against everything that had tried to take them from her.

From Amira, I learned that tenderness is not softness. It is choosing to describe the jasmine when you could describe the rubble.

These women did not set out to teach me anything. Emma was just being stubborn and certain. Lucia was just being open and unashamed. Amira was just being tender and precise. But I was watching, as I have always watched, and what I saw in each of them was a different answer to the same question: How do you move through the world as yourself?

Bravery. Vulnerability. Tenderness. These are the words I have borrowed from the women who lent them to me.

I think that is what identity is — not something you discover whole, like a fossil in rock, but something you build from the materials the people you love leave lying around.
